title: The doorway to
a
healthier
heart.
lead: Notes from the surgeon's pen — heart health, surgical journeys and patient stories.

empty.title: No results
empty.body: Try a different search or category.

midcab.title: What is MIDCAB? A guide to minimally invasive coronary bypass
midcab.excerpt: How a 5–8 cm incision below the left breast replaces the traditional sternotomy — and which patients are good candidates.

body.h2: Coronary bypass without opening the breastbone

body.p: In classic coronary bypass, the surgeon divides the breastbone (sternum) along the midline. MIDCAB — Minimally Invasive Direct Coronary Artery Bypass — skips that step. A 5–8 cm incision in the left chest wall, just below the breast, is enough.

The left internal mammary artery (LIMA) is sewn directly to the left anterior descending coronary artery (LAD), restoring flow past the blockage. In most cases the heart-lung machine isn't needed; the operation is performed on a beating heart.
MIDCAB isn't the right choice for every bypass candidate. The patients we see the best results in tend to fit one of these profiles:

ul: Single-vessel disease: A significant blockage limited to the LAD.
Hybrid candidates: Multi-vessel disease where MIDCAB to the LAD is combined with stenting (PCI) of the other vessels.
High open-surgery risk: Older patients, lung disease, or prior chest surgery.

body.p: Every patient's anatomy is different. The decision is made by reviewing your coronary angiogram, echocardiogram, and chest CT together.

ul: Faster recovery: Without a sternotomy, early-week pain is markedly less. Most patients return to full activity in 4–6 weeks; classic bypass takes 8–12 weeks.
Less blood loss: A smaller surgical field reduces transfusion need.
Shorter hospital stay: Typically 3–4 days, versus 5–7 for classic bypass.
Smaller scar: Hidden under clothing — and quite cosmetic.

body.p: The biggest limitation of MIDCAB is access. The left chest incision exposes the LAD area directly, but circumflex or right coronary artery grafting requires a different technique. That's when a hybrid surgical-interventional approach comes into play.
Surgeon experience matters. Working through a small incision is technically harder, so MIDCAB should be performed in high-volume, experienced centres.
Surgery typically lasts 2–3 hours. You spend a brief time in intensive care, then move to a regular ward. Drains are removed within 1–2 days; discharge is usually on day 3 or 4.
In the first week at home you avoid heavy lifting, driving, and long travel. Desk work resumes around week 6; physical work and exercise gradually return at 8–12 weeks.

If you've been told you need bypass surgery, or your angiogram shows a significant blockage, take the time to review your options carefully — don't rush. Is MIDCAB a good fit for you? Bring your imaging to a consultation and we'll go through it together to identify the most appropriate approach for your case.

off-pump-vs-on-pump.title: Off-pump vs. on-pump bypass: which approach, when?
off-pump-vs-on-pump.excerpt: We compare the trade-offs of the heart-lung machine versus operating on a beating heart.

body.p: Coronary bypass surgery has one objective: open a new path beyond a blocked vessel. There are two main ways to get there.
In on-pump bypass, the heart is briefly stopped and a heart-lung machine takes over circulation. The surgeon sews into a still, motionless heart.
In off-pump bypass (beating-heart bypass), the heart keeps working. Specialised stabilisers immobilise only the small area being sutured. The body's own circulation is preserved throughout.
Both have evidence-based roles. Choice depends on anatomy, comorbidities, and surgeon experience.

ul: Controlled field: Operating on a still heart maximises suture precision.
Better in complex anatomy: Multi-vessel disease benefits from unrestricted access.
Long-term patency data: Decades of follow-up support its outcomes.
Pump-free physiology: Avoids the inflammation and embolic load associated with cardiopulmonary bypass.
Kidney-protective: Reduces dialysis risk in patients with borderline renal function.
Neurological advantage: Some studies show a small but meaningful drop in stroke rate.
Less transfusion: No extracorporeal circuit means lower blood loss.

body.p: Roughly:

ul: High-risk, multiple comorbidities: Often off-pump.
Multi-vessel, complex anatomy: Often on-pump.
Younger, lower-risk: Both work well; surgeon experience tips the balance.
Calcified aorta: "No-touch" off-pump avoids aortic manipulation.

body.p: No. Both approaches use the same sternotomy. "Minimally invasive" applies to techniques like MIDCAB, where the breastbone is preserved.
It depends on the surgeon. Single-graft cases are similar; multi-vessel off-pump cases can take longer.
Modern heart-lung machines are not those of 30 years ago. In the right patient, on-pump doesn't worsen outcomes.
In consultation we review your angiogram, echocardiogram, and lab values together. The decision is shaped by:

ul: Number of diseased vessels and graft target quality
Left ventricular function
Renal function
Aortic calcification
Prior chest surgery

body.p: Nothing is "always better." What matters is choosing the right evidence-based approach for you.

If bypass surgery is on the table and you're unsure which approach fits, book a consultation. With your imaging in front of us, we'll calmly walk through why one option is preferred over the other in your case.

bypass-recovery.title: Recovering from bypass: the first 12 weeks
bypass-recovery.excerpt: What to do, what to avoid, and when to return to work and to social life after bypass surgery.

body.p: The first 12–24 hours after bypass surgery are typically spent in intensive care. During this time:

ul: Breathing is initially supported by a ventilator; in most patients the tube comes out within hours.
Cardiac rhythm is monitored continuously.
Chest drains (small tubes) prevent fluid build-up; they're removed within 1–2 days.
Pain is managed actively — modern protocols make this far more comfortable than people expect.

body.p: Once you move from intensive care to a ward, mobilisation begins. The first goal: sitting at the bedside, then short walks in the corridor. Breathing exercises (incentive spirometry) are central — they significantly reduce lung complications.
Diet starts with fluids and progresses to normal food. Average hospital stay is 5–7 days; for minimally invasive procedures (MIDCAB, sutureless TAVI) it can be as short as 3–4 days.
When you're discharged, things won't feel "normal" — and that's expected:

ul: Fatigue: The most common complaint. Energy goes inward to heal. Short naps during the day are fine.
Sternum sensations: After classic bypass, breastbone tightness and tenderness last 6–8 weeks. With MIDCAB, this is much milder.
Sleep disruption: It takes a few days to leave hospital rhythm behind.
Mood: Mild low mood or anxiety in the first 4–6 weeks is common. If it doesn't lift, please tell us.

body.p: This is the period to gradually extend daily walks. The rule of thumb: walk at a pace where you can still talk without getting breathless.

ul: Driving: Wait 4–6 weeks after classic bypass; 2–3 weeks after MIDCAB.
Sexual activity: Generally safe once you can climb a flight of stairs comfortably (around week 4).
Lifting: Stay under 5 kg for the first six weeks. Strict adherence allows the sternum to fully heal.

body.h2: Weeks 6–12: back to work and life

ul: Desk-based work: Safe to return at week 6.
Physical work: Reviewed at week 10–12 with your doctor.
Exercise: Walking and low-intensity activity, building to normal exercise by month three.
Cardiac rehabilitation: If offered, attend. Structured exercise, education, and psychological support measurably improve one-year outcomes.

body.p: Bypass solves the blockage but doesn't cure the underlying vascular disease. To keep your grafts patent for the long term:

ul: Statins: Almost always lifelong.
Antiplatelet therapy: Aspirin is core; anything added is decided by your surgical team.
Smoking: Must stop. The strongest single predictor of graft failure.
Blood pressure and glucose: Stay on top of targets with your cardiologist.
Diet and exercise: A Mediterranean-style diet and 150 minutes of moderate activity per week.

body.p: Don't wait if any of these appear:

ul: New chest pain
Severe shortness of breath
Wound discharge, redness, or fever
Sudden one-sided swelling (especially in a leg)
Palpitations or fainting

body.p: Recovery is different for every patient. If anything is worrying you, contact us — don't wait. Book a follow-up appointment, or message us quickly via WhatsApp.

classic-vs-minimal-bypass.title: Classic open bypass vs. minimally invasive: which fits which patient?
classic-vs-minimal-bypass.excerpt: Evidence-based outcomes, anatomy, and patient profile — we walk through how the choice is actually made.

body.p: In coronary bypass surgery the goal is simple: restore blood flow past a blockage. Classic bypass and minimally invasive bypass (MIDCAB and similar variants) reach that goal through different doors.
In classic (sternotomy) bypass, the surgeon divides the breastbone in the midline. This access:

ul: Reaches every coronary artery directly.
Is the safest approach in multi-vessel disease.
In emergencies, gives the team rapid access to all instruments.
Is supported by decades of long-term follow-up data.

body.p: When the aorta, valves, and multiple vessels need to be addressed in one session, classic bypass remains the gold standard.

body.h2: Minimally invasive: superior in the right patient

body.p: MIDCAB and similar techniques work through 5–8 cm incisions. The breastbone is preserved; the surgeon works between chest-wall muscles.

ul: Faster recovery: Most patients return to full activity in 4–6 weeks.
Less pain: Sternum integrity intact means dramatically less early-week discomfort.
Cosmetic edge: The scar sits beneath bra-strap or t-shirt line.
Shorter stay: 3–4 days versus 5–7 for classic.

body.p: But minimally invasive isn't for everyone.

ul: Single-vessel (LAD) disease — MIDCAB
Multi-vessel, complex anatomy — Classic bypass
Concurrent valve or aortic surgery — Classic bypass
Older age, multiple comorbidities, high open-surgery risk — Hybrid (MIDCAB + stent) or heart team review
Redo surgery (prior bypass) — Case by case; minimally invasive can be advantageous

body.p: In experienced hands, long-term outcomes are very similar between the two. With the right patient selection, five-year graft patency and survival rates are not clinically different.
The clear differences are in early comfort and recovery speed. For an active, working person — or someone with single-vessel disease — the minimally invasive advantages are hard to ignore.

At our practice in Antalya every case is, where possible, reviewed in a heart team huddle (surgeon, cardiologist, anesthetist). Imaging, labs, and the patient's lifestyle priorities are discussed together.
Key questions:

ul: What does the anatomy permit?
Will any concurrent surgery be needed?
What are the patient's personal recovery goals?
Do comorbidities limit the minimally invasive option?

body.p: The right question is: "Which is better for me?" — and that answer is patient-specific.
Bring your angiogram report to a consultation and we'll walk through every option clearly. The decision is always yours; our job is to lay out the choices honestly.

tavi.title: What is TAVI? Aortic valve replacement without open surgery
tavi.excerpt: A new-generation aortic valve, delivered through a groin artery — designed for older or higher-risk patients.

body.h2: An alternative to open surgery for aortic stenosis

body.p: TAVI — Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation — is a catheter-based valve replacement for severe aortic stenosis, performed without open-heart surgery. First introduced in 2002, it has become the standard of care for many older patients over the past two decades.

During the procedure a new biological valve, compressed inside a thin catheter system, is inserted via the femoral artery in the groin. It is advanced up the aorta, positioned inside the diseased valve, and expanded. The old valve isn't removed — the new one starts working from inside it.
In its early years TAVI was offered only to patients at very high surgical risk. Indications have widened considerably:

ul: Patients over 75 with severe aortic stenosis (routine indication)
High surgical risk (lung disease, kidney failure, frailty)
Prior chest surgery
Selected younger, anatomically favourable intermediate-risk patients (case by case)

body.p: The decision is always made by a heart team (surgeon, cardiologist, anesthetist, imaging specialist). Aortic annulus dimensions, calcification pattern, coronary ostia distance, and access vessel quality are reviewed in detail on CT angiography.

ul: Incision — TAVI: small puncture in the groin · Open AVR: sternotomy
Anaesthesia — TAVI: often conscious sedation · Open AVR: general required
Hospital stay — TAVI: 2–4 days · Open AVR: 5–7 days
Recovery — TAVI: 1–2 weeks · Open AVR: 6–8 weeks
Valve durability (younger) — TAVI: 8–12+ years · Open AVR: 15–20 years (bioprosthesis)

body.p: Most patients receive light sedation; general anaesthesia is available if needed. The procedure takes 60–90 minutes in the cath lab, with continuous fluoroscopy guiding valve positioning.
The first night is spent in a coronary care unit. Most patients stand the next day and are discharged within 2–4 days.
TAVI is a high-success procedure but not without risk:

ul: Pacemaker need: The most common complication (5–15%). Some patients need a permanent pacemaker afterwards.
Vascular injury: Femoral access may need surgical repair.
Stroke: Low (2–4%) but a meaningful risk.
Paravalvular leak: Small leaks between the new valve and the aortic wall — most resolve on their own or are well tolerated.

body.p: Antiplatelet therapy is taken for six months. Prophylactic antibiotics may be needed before dental work. Annual echocardiography monitors valve function.
Most patients feel a significant improvement in shortness of breath and chest pain — quality of life rises markedly.

If you have new shortness of breath, exertional chest pressure, or fainting episodes, and an echocardiogram shows severe aortic stenosis, the decision shouldn't be rushed. Bring your imaging to a consultation and we'll review whether TAVI is the right option for you, together.

sutureless-aortic.title: Sutureless aortic valves: a faster, suture-free biological valve
sutureless-aortic.excerpt: By eliminating suturing time, this approach shortens cross-clamp time and offers an alternative for higher-risk surgical candidates.

body.h2: A new-generation aortic valve that anchors without sutures

body.p: In classic aortic valve replacement the surgeon, after removing the diseased valve, secures the new biological or mechanical valve with 12–15 individual sutures. This step is technically demanding and adds time.

Sutureless aortic valves remove that step. The valve sits inside a special stent frame; once positioned, the elastic stent anchors the valve to the aortic annulus automatically. Just three guiding sutures are required.
The shift sounds small, but it shortens operating time meaningfully:

ul: Aortic cross-clamp time: Heart-stopped time is 30–40% shorter than classic AVR.
Heart-lung machine time: Drops by a similar margin.
Total operative time: About 30–45 minutes shorter.

body.p: Shorter pump time reduces lung and kidney complications — particularly for older or comorbid patients.
Sutureless aortic valves stand out in three scenarios:

ul: High surgical risk: Patients near the limit for open AVR but not ideal for TAVI.
Concurrent surgery: Bypass + valve or multi-valve operations, where shorter pump time matters most.
Minimally invasive access: Right upper mini-thoracotomy, where suturing in a small field is challenging — sutureless makes the operation feasible.

body.h2: How is it different from TAVI?

body.p: The two are sometimes confused:

ul: Approach — Sutureless AVR: open surgery (mini- or full sternotomy) · TAVI: catheter (femoral)
Diseased valve — Sutureless AVR: removed · TAVI: crushed, left in place
Cross-clamp — Sutureless AVR: yes (short) · TAVI: no
Estimated valve life — Sutureless AVR: 15–20 years · TAVI: 10–12 years (long-term data limited)
Calcification tolerance — Sutureless AVR: more flexible · TAVI: limited in certain patterns

body.p: Think of sutureless as "the surgery closest to TAVI." The two aren't interchangeable; each has its place.
Multi-centre studies in experienced hands report:

ul: 30-day mortality: 1–2% (similar to classic AVR)
Permanent pacemaker need: 8–10% (lower than TAVI)
Hospitalisation: 4–6 days
5-year valve dysfunction: very low

body.p: No procedure is perfect:

ul: Slightly higher pacemaker rate than conventional valves.
Anatomic restrictions: wide aortic annulus or specific calcification patterns may rule it out.
Cost: higher than a conventional bioprosthesis.

body.p: In consultation we review echocardiography, coronary angiography, and CT angiography (when needed) together. Your surgical risk score, anatomy, and any concurrent surgical needs are weighed jointly.

If you've been diagnosed with aortic valve disease and the question "open AVR or TAVI?" hasn't produced a clear answer, sutureless can be a third door. Bring your imaging to a consultation and we'll review options together.

mitral-repair-vs-replacement.title: Mitral valve: repair or replace?
mitral-repair-vs-replacement.excerpt: The long-term advantages of preserving your own valve, and the situations where replacement becomes the right call.

body.p: The mitral valve is a one-way valve between the left atrium and left ventricle. When it fails to close properly blood flows backwards (mitral regurgitation); when it narrows, flow forwards is impaired (mitral stenosis).
Treatment options split into two paths: repair the existing valve or replace it with a new one. The core principle of modern cardiac surgery: repair, whenever possible.
Preserving the patient's own valve has measurable long-term advantages:

ul: Better left ventricular function: Native valve mechanics are preserved.
No anticoagulation: A mechanical valve requires lifelong warfarin; repair usually does not.
Lower endocarditis (infection) risk.
Better long-term survival: Studies consistently show longer survival after successful repair than after replacement.

body.p: Success depends on the type of valve disease:

ul: Degenerative MR (Barlow / FED): Repair rate over 95%. Modern techniques (neochords, annuloplasty ring, triangular resection) are highly effective.
Functional MR (heart-failure related): Possible but with more variable long-term results.
Rheumatic mitral stenosis: Durable repair is rare; commissurotomy may be tried in selected cases.
Endocarditis: Small vegetations — repair; widespread destruction — replacement.

body.p: Key surgical tools:

ul: Annuloplasty ring: Restores the dilated annulus to its anatomic shape.
Neochords (artificial chordae tendineae): ePTFE strands replace ruptured or elongated native chords.
Leaflet resection / re-shaping: Excess tissue on the posterior leaflet is removed.
Edge-to-edge repair: In selected cases the two leaflets are joined.

body.p: Replacement is the right choice when:

ul: Severe leaflet calcification
Widespread rheumatic damage
Extensive tissue loss after endocarditis
An intra-operative TEE shows unsatisfactory repair (we then convert to replacement)

body.p: Two valve options:

ul: Mechanical: Highly durable (40+ years), but lifelong anticoagulation required.
Biological (tissue): No anticoagulation needed, but may require redo replacement at 10–15 years. Preferred in older patients.

body.p: Mitral surgery today is often performed through a right mini-thoracotomy — without dividing the breastbone. A 5–6 cm incision below the right breast is enough. The advantages:

ul: Less pain
4–5 day hospital stay
Return to normal activity in 3–4 weeks

body.p: Repair success rates are equivalent to conventional sternotomy.

Repair gives the best results when the valve is still "salvageable." Waiting too long can cause irreversible left-ventricular damage. Modern guidelines recommend earlier surgery for symptomatic severe regurgitation — particularly when high repair success is expected.

If you've been diagnosed with mitral regurgitation and surgery is on the table, the first priority is understanding whether your anatomy is repairable. Bring your echocardiogram to a consultation for a detailed review.

valve-disease-signs.title: Heart valve disease: symptoms you shouldn't ignore
valve-disease-signs.excerpt: Breathlessness, palpitations, ankle swelling — which symptoms suggest valve disease, and when should you see a specialist?

body.h2: It starts quietly, then narrows your life

body.p: Heart valves keep blood moving in the right direction through the four chambers. A valve either narrows (restricting flow) or fails to close completely (allowing backflow). Either way, the heart's workload rises.
The catch: valve disease usually progresses silently for years. Symptoms creep in gradually and are easy to dismiss as "ageing" or "being out of shape."

Stairs, slopes, or short walks that didn't bother you before — now leaving you breathless? That's the most common first sign of valve disease. As left-heart pressures rise, fluid backs up into the lungs.
Beyond ordinary tiredness — needing to sit down after shorter activities than before suggests reduced cardiac output.
An irregular heartbeat may be the first sign of atrial fibrillation. Mitral disease significantly raises AF risk.
If your sock marks are deeper at night, or your shoes feel tighter — right-heart strain may be the cause.
Classic in aortic stenosis. The cause isn't the coronaries — it's the heart muscle itself struggling to get enough blood.
The most serious aortic-stenosis symptom. Don't dismiss exertional fainting — it shouldn't wait.
If you can't sleep flat and need extra pillows — left-heart failure is advanced.

ul: Exertional chest pain + fainting — Aortic stenosis
Breathlessness lying flat + ankle swelling — Mitral regurgitation
Palpitations + fatigue — Mitral disease
Dyspnoea + right-heart signs — Tricuspid regurgitation

body.p: A single symptom never makes the diagnosis. Echocardiography clarifies which valve is involved and how severely.

body.h2: Murmurs — what they are and aren't

body.p: Has your doctor heard a "murmur"? A murmur is the sound of blood crossing a valve or vessel. Not every murmur means disease:

ul: Innocent murmur: Common in children, pregnancy, fever.
Pathologic murmur: Suggests valve narrowing, regurgitation, or congenital anomaly.

body.p: Any newly identified murmur should be evaluated with echocardiography.
Don't wait if you have any of:

ul: Marked fatigue with previously easy activities
Recurring palpitations
Exertional chest pain, pressure, or fainting
New ankle swelling
Breathlessness lying flat

body.p: Go to the emergency room for any of:

ul: Severe, persistent chest pain
Sudden severe shortness of breath
Sudden loss of consciousness

body.p: Usually two steps:

ul: Clinical exam + ECG: Murmur, rhythm, heart size
Echocardiography: Detailed valve structure, motion, and leak severity

body.p: In complex cases, transoesophageal echocardiography or cardiac MRI/CT may be added.

If one or more of the symptoms above describe you, a simple echocardiogram usually answers most questions quickly. Don't hesitate to book a check-up — the earlier valve disease is detected, the wider your surgical options remain.

aortic-aneurysm-guide.title: Aortic aneurysm: from diagnosis to treatment
aortic-aneurysm-guide.excerpt: A silent condition that can become life-threatening. Screening, follow-up, and the surgical decision.

body.p: The aorta is the body's main artery — leaving the heart and travelling down through the abdomen to supply every organ. An aneurysm is a weakening of part of the aortic wall that balloons outward beyond its normal diameter.
By location:

ul: Ascending aortic aneurysm: Just above the heart.
Aortic arch aneurysm: The curved section where the brain's vessels branch off.
Descending thoracic aneurysm: Within the chest.
Abdominal aneurysm: In the abdomen.

body.p: This article focuses on thoracic aortic aneurysms.
Usually not. Aneurysms are typically found incidentally on imaging done for another reason (chest CT, echocardiogram).
When symptoms do appear, they may include:

ul: Deep, dull pain between the shoulder blades or in the front of the chest
Hoarseness (an enlarging aorta can press on the left vocal nerve)
Difficulty swallowing
Aortic valve regurgitation symptoms (when the valve is also affected)

body.p: Sudden, "tearing" chest or back pain is always an emergency — it may indicate dissection or rupture.

ul: High blood pressure (the strongest single factor)
Smoking
Family history (especially first-degree relatives with aneurysm)
Genetic syndromes: Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos, Loeys-Dietz, Turner
Bicuspid aortic valve
Older age

body.p: Anyone with a first-degree relative affected by thoracic aortic aneurysm should be screened once with echocardiography or CT. In Marfan and similar genetic conditions, annual surveillance is standard.
Aneurysm diameter directly affects rupture risk:

ul: < 4 cm — Very low annual rupture/dissection risk
4–4.9 cm — < 1% annual risk
5–5.9 cm — 5–10% annual risk
≥ 6 cm — 15%+ annual risk

body.p: Surgery is generally recommended at 5.5 cm; in Marfan or bicuspid valve cases the threshold drops to 5 cm or even 4.5 cm. Rapid growth (more than 0.5 cm per year) is an independent indication for surgery.
By location and size:

ul: Ascending aorta: Replacement with a prosthetic graft. Bentall or David is added based on valve status.
Aortic arch: Complex; often requires deep hypothermic circulatory arrest.
Descending thoracic: TEVAR (endovascular stent-graft) or open surgery.
Abdominal: EVAR (endovascular) or open surgery.

body.p: Open ascending aortic surgery typically requires 6–8 days in hospital. Full recovery takes 2–3 months. The graft itself is permanent and is monitored long-term with periodic echocardiography or CT.
For aneurysms below 5.5 cm, annual CT or MR follow-up is sufficient. During this period:

ul: Blood pressure control is critical (target < 130/80 mmHg)
Stop smoking
Beta-blockers are recommended in most patients
Avoid heavy isometric exercise (powerlifting, bodybuilding)
Continue aerobic exercise (walking, swimming — beneficial)

body.p: If your imaging report mentions "ascending aortic ectasia" or "aortic aneurysm," book a consultation rather than worrying. Once we know the size, growth rate, and any associated conditions, the follow-up and treatment plan becomes straightforward.

aortic-dissection-emergency.title: Aortic dissection: an emergency where minutes matter
aortic-dissection-emergency.excerpt: What a Type A dissection is, why it requires surgery within hours, and how our 24/7 team is structured around it.

body.p: Aortic dissection is a tear in the inner layer of the aortic wall, allowing blood to flow between the layers. This creates a second channel running alongside the true lumen.
It splits into two types:

ul: Type A: Involves the ascending aorta — emergency surgery. Untreated mortality rises 1–2% per hour.
Type B: Begins beyond the left subclavian artery — initially treated medically.

body.p: This article focuses on Type A — the form that is most rapidly life-threatening.
The classic picture:

ul: Sudden, "tearing" chest pain — often radiating to the back
Confusion (if brain perfusion is affected)
Pulse loss in one arm or a pulse difference between arms
Fainting
Stroke-like symptoms
Abdominal pain (if mesenteric arteries are involved)

body.p: Dissection is often confused with a heart attack. The pain character differs: heart attack pain is pressure-like and builds gradually; dissection pain is sudden and described as "tearing."

ul: Uncontrolled hypertension (the most common single cause)
Existing aortic aneurysm
Genetic syndromes (Marfan, Ehlers-Danlos)
Bicuspid aortic valve
Trauma (especially high-speed motor vehicle collision)
Cocaine use
Pregnancy (especially the third trimester in Marfan patients)

body.p: Suspected dissection:

ul: Rapid contrast-enhanced chest CT — the gold standard.
Transoesophageal echocardiography (TEE) is a faster alternative when the patient is unstable.

body.p: If suspicion is strong, treatment is started before imaging completes.
Type A → operating room immediately:

ul: Target blood pressure < 110 mmHg and pulse ~60 bpm (IV beta-blocker + nitroglycerine)
Pain management (morphine)
Blood products prepared
Surgery typically under deep hypothermic circulatory arrest
Ascending aorta replaced with a tube graft; valve and root addressed if needed

body.p: Surgery is complex and long (5–7 hours). Mortality correlates directly with time to surgery; survival is significantly higher in patients operated within 24 hours.

At our practice, dissection cases are a priority. The contracted-hospital cardiac team is on call around the clock. CT scan, anaesthesia, and operating room logistics are completed within 60–90 minutes — a window in which many centres are still in the initial assessment phase.
Two to four days in intensive care, then a regular ward. Total hospital stay is typically 10–14 days. Recovery is longer than after standard cardiac surgery: full recovery takes 3–4 months.
Patients who have survived aortic dissection require:

ul: Lifelong annual CT or MR (remaining aortic segments may dilate over time)
Strict blood-pressure control (< 130/80 mmHg, often < 120/80 is better)
Avoidance of heavy physical strain
Genetic screening for relatives, when appropriate

body.p: If you have sudden, unusually severe chest or back pain, do not attempt to manage it yourself — go directly to the emergency department. Even a one-hour delay can worsen outcomes.
If you have a known aortic aneurysm, or relatives with prior dissection, periodic echocardiography and CT screening are advisable. Contact us to book a consultation.

bentall-vs-david.title: Bentall vs. David: choosing between two root procedures
bentall-vs-david.excerpt: Should the aortic valve be preserved, or replaced together with the root? We make the decision based on anatomy and long-term goals.

body.h2: Two classic procedures for the aortic root

body.p: The aortic root sits just above the heart and contains the aortic valve and the origins of the coronary arteries. When this region is diseased (dilation, aneurysm) and surgery is required, there are two principal options:

ul: Bentall procedure: Replace the aortic valve, root, and ascending aorta together.
David procedure: Preserve the patient's own aortic valve; replace only the root and ascending aorta.

body.p: Both are valid, evidence-based options. The choice depends on the valve's condition and the patient's anatomy.
Described in 1968 by Hugh Bentall, this procedure:

ul: Removes the diseased aortic valve
Inserts a composite valved graft (a prosthetic valve combined with the aortic tube graft)
Reimplants the coronary ostia onto the new graft

body.p: Result: a single replacement of valve + root + aorta.

ul: The aortic valve is significantly damaged (calcification, rheumatic damage, endocarditis)
Bicuspid aortic valve with marked regurgitation
The valve cannot be repaired due to a structural problem
Proven technique: 50+ years of experience
Single-stage solution: Valve and root together
Shorter operative time than David
Mechanical valve: lifelong anticoagulation required
Biological valve: redo surgery often needed in 10–15 years
Any future issue (e.g., endocarditis) affects the entire construct

body.p: Described in 1992 by Tirone David, this technique:

ul: Leaves the aortic valve in place (when the leaflets are healthy)
Replaces the diseased root and ascending aorta with a tube graft
Re-suspends the aortic valve inside the new graft

body.p: Result: the patient's own valve is preserved, only the root structure is renewed.

ul: Root aneurysm with structurally healthy aortic leaflets
Younger patients
When anticoagulation is to be avoided
Marfan syndrome (most of these patients are young)
No anticoagulation: the native valve is preserved
Lower endocarditis risk
Better hemodynamics: natural valve flow pattern
Better long-term survival: studies favour valve-sparing approaches
Technically more demanding: surgeon experience matters
Longer operative time
Valve regurgitation may develop: 5–10% may need reintervention over years
Not suitable for everyone: if leaflets are degenerated, David is not feasible

body.p: Key factors in consultation:

ul: Age — Older favours Bentall · Younger favours David
Valve status — Marked damage favours Bentall · Leaflets healthy favours David
Anticoagulation suitability — Acceptable favours Bentall · To be avoided favours David
Genetic syndrome — Absent favours Bentall · Marfan etc. favours David
Surgeon experience — Standard favours Bentall · High-volume favours David

body.p: Often the final decision is made intra-operatively with TEE: if the leaflets are genuinely healthy, David proceeds; if they are not adequate, Bentall is performed. This safety net protects the patient regardless of scenario.
Both procedures yield excellent results in experienced hands:

ul: 30-day mortality: 1–3% (both procedures)
5-year survival: 85–90%+
Reoperation: Bentall depends on valve durability; David ~1% per year

body.p: If you have aortic root disease and surgery is on the table, bring your imaging (CT angiography, echocardiogram) for a joint review. Whether your leaflets are repairable usually becomes clear quickly — and that answers the key question shaping the operative plan.

living-with-aneurysm.title: Living with an aneurysm: surveillance, lifestyle, control
living-with-aneurysm.excerpt: Follow-up intervals, blood-pressure management, and daily-life recommendations for aneurysms that don't yet need surgery.

body.p: The discovery of a small aortic aneurysm on imaging is initially worrying. The good news: most small aneurysms don't require surgery for years. With the right surveillance and lifestyle decisions, things can stay stable for a long time.
This article is a roadmap for patients with an aneurysm that hasn't reached the surgical threshold.
Frequency depends on size and location:

ul: 4.0–4.4 cm — Annual echocardiography
4.5–4.9 cm — Annual echo or CT/MR
5.0–5.4 cm — Every 6 months
5.5 cm and above — Surgical consultation

body.p: In Marfan or related genetic syndromes the threshold is lower (5 cm, even 4.5 cm). Growth of more than 0.5 cm per year shifts surgery forward.
The single strongest factor influencing aneurysm growth is blood pressure. Targets:

ul: General: < 130/80 mmHg
Marfan or high risk: < 120/80 mmHg

body.p: For control:

ul: Beta-blockers are first-line (reduce heart rate and aortic wall stress)
ARBs (e.g., losartan) can add protection in Marfan
Diuretics and calcium-channel blockers can help

body.p: Home monitoring: morning and evening, several times a week. Share consistent highs with your cardiologist.

body.h2: Exercise: yes, but the right kind

body.p: Living with an aneurysm doesn't mean inactivity. Regular aerobic exercise actually slows aneurysm growth.

ul: Walking, hiking
Swimming
Light cycling
Yoga (avoiding breath-holding and Valsalva manoeuvres)
Light weights with high reps (under 5 kg)
Heavy weightlifting, bodybuilding (heavy isometrics spike blood pressure)
Strength sports: wrestling, powerlifting, heavy CrossFit
High-contact sports: boxing, judo
Explosive sports: sprinting, intense racket play
Diving (especially scuba)

body.p: Rule of thumb: avoid activities involving breath-holding or sudden pressure changes.
Smoking directly damages the aortic wall structure and doubles the growth rate. It is the single strongest modifiable factor.

ul: Mediterranean-style diet: olive oil, fish, vegetables, fruit, nuts
Salt: under 5 g per day
Alcohol: moderate; avoid amounts that raise blood pressure
Stress management: chronic stress raises BP; meditation and breathing exercises help
Sleep: 7–8 hours; sleep apnoea must be treated (it accelerates aneurysm growth)

body.p: For first-degree relatives:

ul: If a parent, sibling, or child has an aneurysm history, a one-time echocardiogram is recommended
Marfan diagnosis warrants genetic counselling and family screening

body.p: Go to the emergency department immediately for:

ul: Sudden, severe "tearing" chest or back pain
Sudden fainting
Stroke-like symptoms (one-sided numbness, speech difficulty)
Sudden weakening of the pulse in one arm

body.p: These suggest dissection or rupture. The decision to go is made in seconds, not minutes.
Living with an aneurysm produces some anxiety in the first year. Most patients adjust over time. When you need it:

ul: Talk with a cardiologist or cardiac surgeon (seeing your numbers concretely helps)
Join an aortic patient community (Marfan foundations, etc.)
Seek a mental health professional if anxiety becomes pronounced

body.p: Are you on a regular surveillance plan? Bring your most recent imaging and current schedule, and we can plan the next step together. Worry isn't the goal — structured surveillance is.

atrial-myxoma.title: Atrial myxoma: the most common benign cardiac tumour
atrial-myxoma.excerpt: Often diagnosed by accident. How it's found, why it must be removed.

body.h2: The most common benign cardiac tumour

body.p: Primary benign tumours of the heart are rare. The most common is atrial myxoma — literally a "myxoid tumour growing in the atrium."
About 75% of atrial myxomas arise in the left atrium and 20% in the right. Most are solitary; in familial forms, multiple lesions can occur.
A classic triad:
The tumour can block blood flow at the mitral valve. This produces:

ul: Exertional shortness of breath
Position-dependent fainting
Pulmonary oedema
Symptoms that ease lying down and worsen when standing

body.p: Because the tumour is mobile, symptoms can change with body position — a classic clue to atrial myxoma.
Fragments break off and enter the circulation. The most common complication:

ul: Stroke (with left atrial myxoma)
Acute peripheral arterial occlusion (e.g., leg ischaemia)
Pulmonary embolism (with right atrial myxoma)

body.p: Tumour-derived cytokines cause:

ul: Fever
Weight loss
Joint pain
Fatigue
Elevated ESR and CRP
Sometimes mistaken for a viral or autoimmune illness

body.p: Often only one of these three patterns dominates — which is why diagnosis is sometimes delayed.
Typically two steps:

ul: Transthoracic echocardiography: Identifies the tumour in over 95% of cases.
Transoesophageal echocardiography (TEE): Clarifies the attachment, stalk, and dimensions.

body.p: CT or MRI may add information, especially in older patients where coronary anatomy needs review in the same session.

body.h2: Surgery: the only option, with excellent results

body.p: Once an atrial myxoma is diagnosed, surgical excision is recommended in every patient. The reasons are:

ul: Risk of embolisation (a little higher every day)
Risk of mechanical obstruction
Rare risk of malignant transformation

body.p: Watchful waiting is not justified.

ul: Standard sternotomy or minimally invasive (right mini-thoracotomy) approach
Heart-lung machine
The tumour is excised with its stalk and a wide cuff of atrial wall
The atrial defect is closed primarily or with a patch
Coexisting mitral regurgitation (from mechanical obstruction) typically resolves
30-day mortality: 1–2%
Local recurrence: 1–3% (higher in familial forms)
Long-term survival: similar to age-matched general population
Hospital stay: 5–7 days

body.p: Annual echocardiography for the first 6–12 months for recurrence surveillance. After that, routine cardiac follow-up suffices in non-familial cases.
Seen in 5–10% of cases. Multiple lesions, younger onset, skin pigmentation, and other endocrine tumours may co-occur. This form requires:

ul: Genetic testing
Mandatory screening of first-degree relatives
More frequent follow-up + earlier surgery

body.p: If imaging has identified an atrial mass, prompt cardiac surgical consultation is recommended. With the right operation, atrial myxomas are essentially curable. Contact us to book a consultation.

maze-procedure.title: The MAZE procedure: surgically ending atrial fibrillation
maze-procedure.excerpt: When fibrillation is long-standing or accompanies other cardiac surgery, surgical ablation can restore sinus rhythm.

body.h2: A surgical solution for atrial fibrillation

body.p: Atrial fibrillation (AF) is the most common rhythm disorder worldwide. Its prevalence rises with age — over a tenth of people aged 65+ live with it. Symptoms include palpitations, fatigue, and exertional shortness of breath. The most serious complication is clot formation in the left atrium leading to stroke.
Treatment options:

ul: Drug-based rhythm/rate control + anticoagulation
Catheter ablation (interventional)
Surgical ablation — the MAZE procedure

body.p: This article focuses on option 3.
Developed in 1987 by Dr. James Cox, the MAZE procedure surgically blocks the abnormal electrical pathways that drive AF.

The original technique is called Cox-Maze; "maze" reflects the labyrinth pattern of incisions that leaves only a single permitted path from sinus node to AV node. All other electrical paths are interrupted.
Modern variants:

ul: Cox-Maze IV: Replaces incisions with radiofrequency or cryoablation lines.
Mini-MAZE: Targets the posterior left atrium and pulmonary veins.

body.p: MAZE is most relevant in:

ul: AF patients undergoing concurrent cardiac surgery: Adding 30–45 minutes during a bypass, valve, or aortic operation.
AF patients in whom catheter ablation has failed
Long-standing persistent AF (where drugs and catheter ablation aren't enough)
Enlarged left atrium (typically > 5–5.5 cm)
History of clot-related stroke — particularly when the left atrial appendage is also closed in the same operation

body.h2: Left atrial appendage — a bonus benefit

body.p: In MAZE the left atrial appendage (LAA) is also surgically closed. Around 90% of AF-related clots originate from this small pouch. Closing it markedly lowers stroke risk — independent of whether the ablation itself maintains sinus rhythm.
When combined with another cardiac operation:

ul: Sternotomy or mini-thoracotomy
Heart-lung machine
Ablation lines placed in the left atrium during the main procedure
LAA stapled or oversewn
30–45 minutes added to the operation

body.p: In stand-alone MAZE (for AF only):

ul: Right mini-thoracotomy (minimally invasive)
Heart-lung machine if needed
2–3 hour operation
Concurrent MAZE: ~75–85% sinus rhythm at one year
Stand-alone MAZE: ~85–90% at one year
Five-year sinus rhythm after catheter ablation: ~50%
Five-year sinus rhythm after MAZE: ~75–85%

body.p: In other words, MAZE produces noticeably more durable results than catheter ablation.

Atrial arrhythmias may persist for the first three months — this is the wound-healing phase. After three months, the rhythm is reassessed; if sinus rhythm is restored, anticoagulation can often be tapered (provided the LAA was also closed).
ECG and Holter monitoring at 3, 6, and 12 months, then annually.

ul: Pacemaker need: 5–10% (ablation near the sinoatrial node)
Bleeding: 2–3%
Stroke (intra-operative): ~1%
Wound infection

body.p: If you're scheduled for cardiac surgery and have AF, MAZE should always be on the agenda. Stand-alone MAZE is also an option for medication-resistant long-standing AF. Bring your treatment history and rhythm records to a consultation for a joint review.

pericardiectomy.title: Pericardiectomy: relieving the constrained heart
pericardiectomy.excerpt: When constrictive pericarditis impairs cardiac filling, removing the pericardium delivers significant relief from breathlessness and oedema.

body.h2: A heart in a "shell": what is constrictive pericarditis?

body.p: The heart sits inside a thin sac called the pericardium. Normally elastic and lubricating, it can — for a number of reasons — become inflamed, thickened, and stiff. The result is a heart effectively trapped inside an armored shell — it contracts but can't fill properly.
The condition is called constrictive pericarditis. It looks like heart failure, but the heart muscle itself is intact. The problem comes from the "outer envelope."
Most common causes:

ul: Tuberculous pericarditis (still important in many regions)
Prior cardiac surgery (with subsequent pericardial thickening)
Prior radiotherapy (especially chest radiation)
Autoimmune disease (rheumatic disease, lupus, vasculitis)
Uraemic pericarditis (advanced kidney failure)
Idiopathic (no identifiable cause)

body.p: In Türkiye, tuberculosis remains a non-trivial cause; rates remain higher in regions with significant migration.
The picture builds slowly over years:

ul: Exertional shortness of breath
Leg swelling, abdominal distention (ascites)
Liver enlargement; jaundice in advanced cases
Distended neck veins
Early satiety, loss of appetite (from liver and splenic congestion)

body.p: In advanced disease patients enter "cardiac cachexia" — losing muscle mass while retaining fluid. This is a late-stage finding and demands attention.
Constrictive pericarditis isn't straightforward to diagnose; it is often confused with restrictive cardiomyopathy (a primary heart-muscle disease). Reliable diagnosis combines:

ul: Echocardiography: Pericardial thickness, septal motion patterns, abrupt early filling
Cardiac MR or CT: Pericardial visualisation (>4 mm thickness is typical)
Right + left heart catheterisation: Characteristic "square root" pressure tracings

body.p: If the diagnosis remains unclear, intra-operative biopsy can help.
Definitive treatment is surgical. The pericardium is:

ul: Removed via sternotomy (classic)
Or via left thoracotomy (older preference)

body.p: Carefully separating the stiff, sometimes calcified pericardium from the heart muscle is technically demanding. Plaques adherent to the myocardium must be peeled away with care.
The extent removed:

ul: Anteriorly: to the sternum
Inferiorly: to the diaphragm
Laterally: to the left phrenic nerve (which is preserved)
Posteriorly: as far as feasible on the right
Immediate relief: Most patients report marked improvement in breathlessness within hours of surgery
Ascites and oedema resolve: within days
30-day mortality: 5–10% (higher in advanced cases)
5-year survival: 75–85%

body.p: The strongest determinant of outcome is timing. Operating before cardiac cachexia develops, and before liver and kidney are heavily affected, dramatically improves results.

ul: 7–10 days in hospital
Normal activity by week six
Full recovery by three months
Diuretics tapered gradually
Underlying causes (TB, rheumatic) treated separately

body.p: Pericardiectomy should be on the agenda when:

ul: Unexplained chronic leg swelling + ascites
Dyspnoea developing after prior cardiac surgery
Tuberculosis history + heart-failure-like symptoms
Heart failure developing late after chest radiotherapy

body.p: If any of these describe you, a cardiac surgery consultation is strongly recommended. Bring your imaging and catheterisation data, and we'll go through it with you.

when-cardiac-surgery.title: When is cardiac surgery necessary? A patient's roadmap
when-cardiac-surgery.excerpt: We clarify the line between conditions managed with medication and those where surgery shouldn't be delayed.

body.p: No. Modern cardiology can manage most heart conditions with medication. Cardiac surgery comes into play when medication isn't enough or when there is a structural problem. This article clarifies the situations where surgery shouldn't be delayed.

Heart tissues undergo irreversible changes over time. A leaking valve left untreated for years lets the left ventricle dilate and lose function. After delayed surgery, the heart may not return fully to its earlier form. Timing is as critical as the surgery itself.

body.h2: Conditions where surgery should be considered

body.p: When medication and stents are not enough, or with:

ul: Left main disease (especially > 50%)
Three-vessel disease + diabetes
Complex anatomy: long, calcified, multiple lesions
Failure of symptom control with medication and stents

body.p: bypass surgery (classic or MIDCAB) is on the table.

ul: Severe aortic stenosis with symptoms (exertional fainting, chest pain, dyspnoea) — don't wait
Severe mitral regurgitation with left-ventricular dilation
Endocarditis — particularly with large vegetations, embolic events, or developing heart failure

body.p: Modern guidelines now recommend earlier surgery even in asymptomatic severe aortic stenosis if left-ventricular function is starting to decline.

ul: Ascending aortic aneurysm ≥ 5.5 cm (5 cm in Marfan; 4.5 cm in some cases)
Type A aortic dissection (emergency)
Rapidly enlarging aneurysm (> 0.5 cm per year)
Significant adult-detected left-to-right shunts (ASD, VSD)
Surgery is most effective before pulmonary hypertension develops
Long-standing AF unresponsive to catheter ablation — MAZE
AF detected during another cardiac operation — add MAZE
Atrial myxoma — surgery in every case
Constrictive pericarditis — pericardiectomy

body.p: If any of these apply, request a cardiac surgical consultation this week:

ul: You've fainted with exertion
You can't sleep flat and need multiple pillows
An echo shows severe aortic stenosis or marked valvular regurgitation
An angiogram shows left main disease
An aneurysm above 5 cm has been detected
You've had prior cardiac surgery and new symptoms have developed

body.h2: Questions to ask before deciding on surgery

body.p: When talking with your doctor, don't hesitate to ask:

ul: What happens if I don't have surgery?
What are my survival numbers with surgery?
Which approach — minimally invasive or classic — fits me?
Can I get a second opinion?
How long does recovery take?

body.p: A good surgeon answers clearly, doesn't become defensive, and supports you in seeking a second opinion.

For a decision as significant as cardiac surgery, a second opinion is reasonable and valuable. We see patients arriving with their existing treatment plans without judgement and review their cases with the same care. We treat second opinions as a natural part of communication.
Is cardiac surgery on the table? Uncertainty causes delay; clarity supports decision-making. Bring your angiogram and echocardiogram, and let's have a calm, evidence-based review together.
